Cisco Systems leads the pack in terms of financial performance. It has $22.8 billion in debt, which is in proportion to its earnings. The company reports net income and free cash flow. Cisco Systems is a bit overvalued compared to its peers. Its market cap is higher than its long-term assets.

NetGear market cap is near its long-term asset value and it trades close to its median price target. The company has improving financial performance. It is undervalued compared to its peers. NetGear has low debt and reports some free cash flow. It often operates at a net loss.

Applied Optoelectronics is overvalued; its market cap is higher than its long-term assets. The company has $46 million in debt and trades below its median target price. The company has good financial performance and its stock has benefited from positive investor sentiment.

AXT is undervalued and has mediocre financial performance. The company operates at a net loss and reports some free cash flow. It has $50 million in short-term debt and $1.7 million in long-term debt. It trades below its book value and median target price.

CommScope Holding is undervalued compared to its peers, but its high debt makes it a risk. The company has excellent financial performance otherwise. It reports net income and some free cash follow. The company’s assets are higher than its market cap. The company’s liabilities outweigh its assets.

CommScope Holding is at low risk of poor financial performance, but a Hold rating for the company comes at a moderate risk to the investor.

Conclusion

CommScope Holding reported improved financial performance for Q1-2025 and expects increased revenues for Q2-2025. The company’s operating segments are increasing due to high demand for its products. The company’s stock price is up significantly over the last twelve months. The company has high debt compared to its income. The company has plans to bring down its debt ratio, but risk abounds for the investor. I rate the company as a Hold for now and recommend that investors watch for future developments from the company.

data.title: JOYY: Deep Value And Amazing Shareholder Returns
data.description:
JOYY Inc. (NASDAQ:JOYY), a video-based social media company, offers a big discount on its net cash pile and generous shareholder returns, despite a stagnant growth outlook. The stock trades at a depressed valuation, partly due to general pessimism about businesses with Chinese origins and links.

Using the dividend discount model (DDM) and assuming zero growth, I value the company at more than twice its current market cap. A similar result can be derived from discounted free cash flows. I’m initiating JOYY coverage with a buy rating and a price target of $89.71, implying a potential upside of more than 100%.

Quick overview

JOYY was founded in 2005 by David Xueling Li, a Chinese journalist-turned-entrepreneur, in South China’s Guangzhou city. The operator of social media platforms has since expanded globally and moved its headquarters to Singapore. Its key brands include live broadcaster Bigo Live, short video platform Likee, social game platform Hago, and instant-messaging app imo.

The company focuses on providing users with real-time “interactive and immersive entertainment”. JOYY derives about one-tenth of its revenue from China, with the rest coming from North America, Europe, the Middle East, Southeast Asia, and other regions, according to the company's 2024 annual report.

Cash pile & cash flow

JOYY is sitting on $3.3b net cash, including long-term deposits and fixed-income investments, its latest quarterly filing shows, while the company has a market cap below $2.3b as of May 22. Excluding goodwill and intangibles, net assets are about $4.3b, implying a 0.5x P/B ratio.

Revenue has declined slightly over the last three years, but operating cash flow has stabilized at around $300m annually. The company’s 2024 net loss was due to a one-off charge of goodwill impairment, which was non-cash and thus had no impact on cash flows.

Annual capex has averaged about $80m over the last three years, with only a small part being maintenance expenditure. Based on the OCF and capex trends, I expect free cash flow to equity to stay above $200m in the short to medium term.

Shareholder returns

Since the start of 2021, JOYY has spent about $1.1b repurchasing stock, reducing its share capital by more than a third. In March 2025, the company announced plans to pay $200m dividends annually over the next three years and to buy back shares for a total of $300m in the same period. Based on its current market cap, JOYY’s combined yield of dividends and buybacks would exceed 13% a year. Generous shareholder returns can be a key to unlocking value for companies trading at steep discounts.

It's worth noting that founder Li remains the top shareholder, with a 35% stake, and hence the biggest beneficiary of continuous dividends and buybacks. Lei Jun, chairman and CEO of Xiaomi Corp. (OTCPK:XIACF), is the No.2 shareholder with an interest of nearly 12%. As JOYY’s angel investor and ex-chairman, Lei has delegated his voting rights to Li.

Valuation

The DDM formula is often used to estimate intrinsic value based on the theory that a company is worth the sum of all its future dividends, discounted back to its present value. It’s appropriate for mature companies with stable dividend payments.

With zero growth assumed, dividends of $200m divided by an 8% cost of equity equal an intrinsic value of $2.5b for JOYY’s ongoing businesses, which adds to the company’s $3.3b net cash for a fair market cap of $5.8b. Applying a 20% margin of safety, I arrive at a price target of $89.71 per share. Alternatively, discounting an annual free cash flow to equity of about $200m would yield a similar result.

More background

JOYY built social media brands YY and Huya (HUYA) and eventually sold them with good timing and pricing. In two transactions in 2020 and 2023, the company sold Huya shares to internet giant Tencent (OTCPK:TCEHY) for a total of $1b. In another deal, JOYY struck an initial agreement with Baidu (BIDU) in late 2020, near the peak of a Chinese tech bubble, to sell YY for $3.6b. The deal was delayed by regulatory hurdles and ultimately revised to close in February this year with a reduced value of $2.1b. Both platforms were sold when they were about to run into development bottlenecks, and Li demonstrated his business acumen in timing these deals.

Key risks

JOYY relies on Bigo Live for a large part of its earnings, but the live-broadcasting platform’s user number and revenue have become stagnant. A decline in Bigo’s business would affect the sustainability of JOYY’s profit and cash flow. Nevertheless, I believe the Bigo risk could mainly hurt the stock’s upside, while the company’s ample cash reserve offers strong protection on the downside.

Secondly, Chinese tech stocks face the threat of forced delisting from US exchanges amid geopolitical tensions. Although JOYY is now based in Singapore with a diverse global revenue, it’s unclear whether the company would still be targeted for its Chinese origin and ties. I see delisting as a remote scenario and have applied a wide margin of safety to JOYY’s valuation. If it happens, it doesn’t mean the end of the world for long-term investors, as there could be relisting on an Asian exchange, probably the Hong Kong bourse.

Most of JOYY’s cash and equivalents are in USD, and the company derives the vast majority of its revenue from outside China. This would help ensure a smooth relisting process if necessary.

Conclusion

JOYY trades at a steep discount to its intrinsic value, partly due to negative sentiment toward Chinese tech names, as well as the company’s stagnant growth outlook. Generous shareholder returns should help unlock JOYY’s deep value, with a combined yield of dividends and buybacks exceeding 13% a year. I value the stock at more than twice its current market cap, based on discounted future dividends and a large cash pile. Key risks include Bigo’s potential downturn and the threat of forced delisting against US-listed Chinese tech stocks in general.
